content.full_text:
Fern PolrrrcAl PnecrrcEs CovrMrssroN
,128 J Street o Suite 620 o Sacramento, CA 95111-2329
(916) 322-5660 r Fax (916) 322-0886
January 13,20lA
Ashlee N. Titus
Bell, McAndrews & Hiltachk, LLP
455 Capitol Mall, Suite 801
Sacramento, CA 95814
Re:
Your Request for Informal Assistance
Our File No. I-10-196
Dear Ms. Titus:
This letter responds to your request for informal assistance on behalf of The California
Agricultural Leadership Foundation ("CALF"). Specifically, yoll request reconsideration or
"retraction" of informal assistance issued to Cory Lund, a policy analyst and project manager for
Western Growers, on the ground that the facts presented by Mr. Lund contained a critical error.
We do not believe it appropriate to revise or retract assistance offered to another person based
upon facts that you believe to be erroneous. The Lundletter (No. I-10-085) was based on facts
presented by the requestor. The Fair Political Practices Commission does not act as a finder of
fact when it renders assistance. (ln re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71.) We can, however, offer
assistance to you on relevant provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act"),1 using the facts
that you now submit. We cannot take a position on differences between your account of the facts
and the facts expressly stated by Mr. Lund. This letter is based solely on the facts you provided.
Because you seek general guidance on the Act, we can offer only informal assistance.2 1td.1
QIJESTION
You ask that we review a planned extension of an existing educational fellowship
program that CALF may offer to management-level employees of certain state agencies, for
compliance with the Act's gift rules.
I The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All staturory
references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political practices
Commission are contained in Sections l8ll0 through 18997 of Title 2 of the Califbrnia Code of Regulations. All
regulatory ret'erences are to Title 2, Division 6 of the Califbrnia Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.
r Infbrmal assistance does not provide the requestor with the immunity provided by an opinion or fbrmal
written advice. (Regulation 18329(c).)


File No. I-10-196
Page No. 2
CONCLUSION
Under your accoLlnt of the facts, the true source of funds used in the fellowship program
administered by CALF would be the program's funding sponsors, and not CALF. Those funds
would be classified as gifts subject to the Act's annual gift limit and reporting requirements,
except to the extent they are used to provide "informational materials"io pro-gram participants.
FACTS
You represent the CALF, a 501(c)(3) nonprofit organization that provides a rwo-year
fellowship program for agriculturalists, with the goal of enhancing the long-term viability of
California agriculture through leadership development. This progru1n p.oiid., z4-31p"iron,
with a two-year fellowship providing over 70 days of training, inctuaing 14 university seminars,
a lO-day national seminar and a 15 day intemational travel seminar. To date, the program has
had over 1,100 graduates.
As an extension of its existing fellowship program, CALF is developing a .,California
Regulatory Agency Fellows Program" to provide government agency manage.i, whose
responsibilities involve policy implementation or interpretation, wittr enhanJed understanding
of the economic consequences of their regulatory actions on California agriculture. The three-
dayltwo-night educational program and tour of California farms and ranches is designed to
deepen the regulators' understanding of food production systems, and to introduce leadership
topics through both classroom and experiential leaming. CALF proposes to provide particip^ants
with travel, lodging and meals for the three day program.
CALF would administer this program and will not contract with Western Growers or any
other person to develop and deliver the program. CALF will control all aspects of the program,
including selection of participants and presenters and establishing the tour itinerary. fo funa *re
program' CALF is soliciting sponsors, including Westem Growers. Sponsors wilinot have any
control or input over which participants are selected, the presenters, or the tour itinerary. CALF
will make all such decisions unilaterally. Sponsors will not be told, in sponsorship solicitations,
the names of program participants. It is likely this information will not be known at the time
sponsors commit to sponsoring or actually do sponsor the program.
ANALYSIS
The kind of program you describe implicates the Act's rules governing gifts to public
officials participating in CALF's program.
The Act provides, at Section 89503(c) rhat:
"No member of a state board or commission or designated
employee of a state or local government agency shall accept gifts
from any single source in any calendar year with a total vaiue of
more than t$+ZO1 if the member or employee would be required to


File No. I-10-196
Page No. 3
report the receipt of income or gifts from that source on his or her
statement of economic interests."3
We presume that the kind of regulatory-agency managers whose responsibilities involve
policy implementation or interpretation would be subject to this annual giftiimit. We unclerstand
i-ts wcll that CALF' a 501(cXl) cor?oration. will firncl tlris program thlotigh clonarions proviclecl
by outside parties with an intetest in Califcrrnia agrictrlture. wlio will not-have control br input in
selecting the public officials invited to participate, persons chosen as program presenters, or the
tour itinerary. Rather, CALF will make these decisions unilaterally.
_ 
Because payments for the travel and lodging associated with this program will be paid by
funds solicited from outside donors, we begin by determining whether the trie source of such
payments would be CALF, which will administer the program, or the donors who actually fund
the program. Regulation 18945, as amended in 20lO,itates the pertinent rule:
"(a) General Rule. A person is the source of a gift if the person makes a gift to an
official and is not acting as an intermediary.
"(b) Gifts through an Intermediary.
"(1) A person is the source of a gift, and a third party is an intermediary of
the gift under Sections 872L0 or 87313, if the person makes a payment to the third
party and the payment is used directly or indirectly by the third party to make a
gift to an official under any of the following conditions:
"(A) The person directs and controls the payment at the time it is used by
the third party to make a gift to the official
"(B) The person and third party have agreed that the payment will be used
by the third party to make a gift to the official.
"(C) The person identifies the official to the third party as the intended
beneficiary of the payment prior to the third party making thepayment to the
official.
"(D) The third party identifies the official to the person as the intended
beneficiary of the payment prior to the person making the payment to the third
party.
"(E) The person knows or has reason to know that the sole or primary
purpose of the payment is to make gifts to officials.
"(F) The official or the official's agent solicits the payment from the
person to the third party for the purpose of making a gift to the official.,'
Given your account of the facts,^Regulation 18945(bXl)(E) controls our analysis.
Program attendees will all be managersbf governmental agencies, whose responsibilities include
interpretation and/or implementation of agency policies. You emphasize that^CALF, not Western
Growers, will administer this program, and that CALF will selecithe particular officials invited
to participate in it, with the identities of actual participants "likely" urirro*n to sponsors when
I The gift limit is adjusted biennially to reflect changes in the Consumer price Index. (Section g9503(0;
Regulation 18940.2.)


File No. I-10-196
Page No. 4
they make their payments. However, since the sponsors will know that all beneficiaries of their
payments will be public officials, it is the sponsors who would be the source of any gifts of travel
and lodging associated with the program administered by CALF. Your account of the facts
clearly anticipates that each sponsor "knows or has reason to know that the sole or primary
ptrlpose of the payment is to make gifts to officials." Under Regulation \B}4i,CALF wii be
acting as the intermediary of these sponsors.4
Pursuant to Regulation 18945(b), CALF's status as a 501(c)(3) corporation does not
qualify its sponsors for the exemption offered by Section 59506(a)(Zt for gifts from 501(c)(3)
entities, if the true source of these gifts are persons that are not 501(c)(3) Jorporations.
Subdivision (b) of Regulation 18945 is a relatively new provision adopteO last year, in part to
clarify the Act's treatment of cases where an ostensible Jource of gifts is a 50licX3) corporation
relying on "the 501(c)(3) exception" at Section 89506(a)(2). Since its adoption, we have written
two advice letters applying ls9a5(b); the calof Advice Letter, No. I-10-10i, and the
Hollingsworth Advice Letter, No. ,{-10-133, copies of which are enclosed for your review.
The " Informational M aterial " Exceptton
The foregoing analysis does not classify as "gifts" all goods and services that may be
supplied to program participants. The Act excludes from its definition of "gift" items classed as
"informational material," a term broadly understood to include "any item *-t i"t serves primarily
to convey information and which is provided for the purpose of assisting the recipient in the
performance of his or her official duties or of the eteitive office he or slie seeks.,i (Section
82028(bX1); Regulation 18942.1.) "Informational material" may include "[b]ooks, reports,
pamphlets, calendars, periodicals, videotapes, or free or discounted admission to informational
conferences or seminars." (Regulation 18942.1(a).) This classification does not, of course,
include payments for travel, which would include costs of transportation, meals or lodging.
(Section 82028(bXl).)
If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.
Sincerely,
Scott Hallabrin
General Counsel
fut@
LTwjgl
By: Lawrence T. Woodlock
'Senior Counsel, Legal Division
o Under Sections 872 l0 and 8't313, an intermediary of a gift to an official is required to provide the
following specitic information to the recipient of the gitt: the nami, address and businesi activity of both the
intermediary and donor. If the value of the gift to a particular participant is prorated among t*o o. more donors, the
intermediary should provide the oftrcial with gift values attributable io each donor.

except to the extent they are used to provide "informational materials"io pro-gram participants.
